text: Robotic iliopubic tract repair of indirect inguinal hernia. There has always been a need to perform inguinal hernia repair without mesh. These repairs are typically performed in open anterior fashion. The posterior approach, while not common, is also described. The first description was by Cheadle in This approach was refined and best described in detail as an established technique by Dr. Lloyd Nihus in

The Nihus repair constituted suture approximation of the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract in order to close inguinal defects. This approach was first described laparoscopically by Gazi-Yerli in However, it was noted that the suturing required was very technically challenging using traditional laparoscopic instruments.

The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and a Foley catheter is inserted. The surgeon uses a varus needle to gain access, followed by the placement of three 8mm robotic ports for the inguinal hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates a right-sided inguinal hernia repair, identifying the round ligament and medial umbilical ligaments. The internal ring with an indirect defect is visible, along with the right inferior epigastric artery.
The surgeon performs dissection and repair using the standard robotic transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) approach, creating a peritoneal flap to access the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon visualizes the iliopubic tract lateral to the inferior epigastric artery. Lateral dissection is performed, clearly revealing the internal ring and indirect defect. The medial space is inspected for any direct or femoral hernia components.

The surgeon completes dissection of the identified defects and visually confirms key anatomical structures before proceeding with the repair. In female patients, the round ligament is routinely sacrificed during this process.
The surgeon identifies the branches of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve during the repair of an indirect defect, ensuring these nerves are visible and not entrapped.

The genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible passing just inferior to the iliopubic tract, while the femoral branch is seen diving laterally and inferior to the iliopubic tract. These nerves are differentiated from vessels and other tubular structures by their typical course.

The surgeon completes the hernia repair by approximating the transversalis arch to the iliopubic tract between the internal ring and the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve. Suturing is performed in an inferior to superior direction using a running barbed suture, ensuring only the transversus abdominis is incorporated to avoid entrapment of the ilioinguinal nerve.

The surgeon completes the repair of both direct and indirect hernia defects by suturing the iliopubic tract to the transversalis arch medially. The peritoneum is then closed using a running barbed absorbable suture.
This concludes our video description of the robotic iliopubic tract repair.
